volume_title: Volume I — The Foundations of Spark
title: The Spark Architecture

blocks.content: Apache Spark is a distributed compute engine. Your Python code runs on one machine (the Driver), but the actual data processing is parallelized across a cluster of Executors. Understanding this boundary — what runs where — is the key to debugging every Spark job you'll ever write.
The 5 components you must know: Driver · DAG Scheduler · Task Scheduler · Cluster Manager · Executors. Every Spark job flows through all five.

The Driver is your Python process — the machine running your Spark application. It hosts the SparkSession, builds the query plan, and coordinates all execution. The Driver never touches data directly; it only schedules work and collects results.

rows: One per application — create once, reuse
Translates your DataFrame API calls into a query tree
Results flow from all executors back to the Driver — can OOM if too large
Driver sends a copy of small DataFrames to every executor (broadcast join)

blocks.content: Never call .collect() on large DataFrames. collect() pulls ALL rows to the Driver's JVM heap. At MAANG scale (billions of rows) this will OOM the Driver and kill your job. Use .limit(N).collect(), .show(), or write to storage.

When you call an action (like .count() or .write), the Driver's DAG Scheduler converts the logical plan into a physical DAG of stages. Each stage is a set of tasks that can run in parallel without a shuffle.

The Task Scheduler then submits tasks to executors via the Cluster Manager. It tracks task completion, re-submits failed tasks (up to spark.task.maxFailures times), and handles speculative execution for stragglers.

rows: Cuts DAG into stages at shuffle boundaries
Driver detects heartbeat timeout, reschedules tasks

blocks.content: The Cluster Manager allocates physical resources (nodes, cores, memory) to Spark. Spark supports three cluster managers: YARN (Hadoop), Kubernetes (cloud-native), and Standalone (built-in). At MAANG, you'll primarily use YARN on-prem or Kubernetes on the cloud.

rows: Cloud (AWS EMR on EKS, GCP Dataproc)
Docker containers, declarative resource requests, autoscaling
No external dependency — SparkMaster manages workers

blocks.content: Executors are JVM processes running on worker nodes. Each executor has a fixed number of CPU cores and a fixed memory allocation. They run tasks (the unit of work), store shuffle intermediate files, and serve as the Spark in-memory cache.

# Executor configuration
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .config('spark.executor.instances', '10')    # 10 executor JVMs
    .config('spark.executor.cores', '4')         # 4 cores each = 40 parallel tasks
    .config('spark.executor.memory', '16g')      # 16 GB heap per executor
    .config('spark.executor.memoryOverhead', '2g') # off-heap (shuffle buffers, etc)
    .getOrCreate()

MAANG rule of thumb for executor sizing: 4-5 cores per executor (balances parallelism vs HDFS throughput), 4 GB per core for memory. Leave 1 core per node for OS/YARN daemons. Never use 1 executor per node with all cores — HDFS can't handle the concurrent connections.

rune_cards.definition: Your Python process. Hosts SparkSession, builds the plan, coordinates execution. Never processes data directly. Results from collect() return here — can OOM.
JVM process on a worker node. Runs tasks, stores shuffle files and cached DataFrames. Fixed cores + memory per executor.
Converts logical query plan into physical stages. Stage boundaries = shuffle boundaries. Each stage = set of tasks that can run in parallel.
The smallest unit of work. One task processes one partition. Number of tasks = number of partitions in the stage.
A set of tasks with no shuffle between them. Stages are separated by shuffle boundaries (groupBy, join, repartition). Stages within a job can sometimes run in parallel.
Allocates physical resources to Spark. YARN (on-prem Hadoop), Kubernetes (cloud), or Standalone (testing). Separate from Spark itself.

quiz.question: A Spark job has 3 stages. Stage 1 has 200 tasks, Stage 2 has 200 tasks (after a shuffle), Stage 3 has 1 task (final reduce). Your cluster has 40 executor cores. About how many waves of tasks run in Stage 1?
options: 200 waves — one task at a time per core
It depends on executor memory, not cores
quiz.explanation: With 40 cores, Spark can run 40 tasks simultaneously. 200 tasks / 40 cores = 5 waves. This is why having more partitions than cores is important — it keeps all cores busy throughout each wave.

quiz.question: A data engineer calls df.collect() on a DataFrame with 50 billion rows. What happens?
options: Spark automatically samples 1 million rows to protect the Driver
All 50 billion rows are transferred to the Driver's JVM heap, very likely causing OutOfMemoryError
Spark writes the result to a temp file and returns a file path
collect() on DataFrames only returns the schema, not the rows
quiz.explanation: collect() pulls EVERY row from all executors to the Driver's JVM heap. At 50B rows, this will OOM the Driver (and probably your cluster's networking too). Use .limit(N).collect() for small samples, or .write.parquet(path) for full output.

quiz.question: Where does a Spark broadcast join place the small table?
options: On the Driver, which all executors query via RPC during the join
In every executor's JVM heap — a copy per executor
In HDFS temp storage, shared by all executors via network reads
In the Cluster Manager memory, dispatched on demand
quiz.explanation: Broadcast joins send a full copy of the small table to EVERY executor's JVM heap. This eliminates shuffle entirely — each executor joins locally against its in-memory copy. The trade-off: the table must fit in executor memory (configured by spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold).
